content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 12, 2017

SMUD CSC
Randall J. Hakes
Senior Attorney & Ethics Officer
6301 S Street
P.O. Box 15830
Sacramento, CA 95852-0830

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-17-138

Dear Mr. Hakes:

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Sacramento Municipal
Utilities District ("SMUD") regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

        Please note that the Commission is not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.
If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us
for additional advice.

                                        QUESTIONS

        SMUD has an opportunity for its employees to participate in an offer from a car
manufacturer to purchase a certain vehicle model at a discounted price, while supplies last. SMUD
would like to know the following:

        1. Is the discount a reportable gift to each participating employee who is obligated to report
gifts from such an entity per SMUD's Conflict of Interest Code?

        2. If the discount is a reportable gift, do any participating employees who have a duty to
disclose such a gift have a conflict of interest requiring some sort of action (the discount exceeds
the State gift limit of $470 per year) if SMUD has no business relationship with the manufacturer
other than the manufacturer having a customer account?

        3. If an elected official at SMUD uses the discount and that official has another position at
another governmental entity which has a business relationship with the manufacturer, would the

___________________
        ¹The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-138
Page No. 2

official be precluded from doing his or her job duties for the other governmental employer as it relates to the manufacturer?

4. Does it matter whether the elected official at SMUD is also an elected appointed official for the other agency or whether he or she is merely an employee of the other agency?

CONCLUSIONS

1-4. Since the discounts are discounts made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status, they are not "gifts" for any purposes.

FACTS

SMUD is a special district in California and provides electricity to Sacramento County and parts of Yolo and Placer counties. SMUD employs approximately 2,100 employees.

The manufacturer is an international manufacturer of automobiles - selling cars throughout the country and the world.

The manufacturer has approached SMUD about offering SMUD's employees a $10,000 discount on the purchase of a new car of a particular model - a discount of not quite 1/3 off the MSRP (manufacturer suggested retail price) of the car under the following circumstances:

• All of SMUD's employees have a chance to participate in this offer (approximately 2,100 employees including approximately 650 officials who file a Form 700), and all public employees at the following University of California campuses: San Diego; Davis, Merced, San Francisco, and Santa Cruz. You noted that the offer is further extended to numerous people outside of SMUD, some of whom are not even public employees and thus could not be considered an official.

• A SMUD employee can participate in the manufacturer's offer by going to a participating dealer to pick out the car they like of that particular model. The employee then shows his or her SMUD employee identification to obtain the discount when purchasing the car from the dealer.

• SMUD understands that the manufacturer pays or credits the dealer in some fashion for the discount that the dealer then passes on to the SMUD employee.

• The offer will run for a limited time, either through the end of June 2017 or while supplies last.

You understand that the manufacturer has made this offer to numerous other entities and their employees have participated. Some of the entities known to be currently participating are Pacific Gas & Electric, Sonoma Clean Power, Kansas City Power, the University of California San Diego, the University of California Davis, the University of California Merced, the University of California San Francisco, and the University of California Santa Cruz. Others have likely participated in the past, but you do not have that information from the manufacturer. As you

File No. A-17-138
Page No. 3

understand it, there are times when the car manufacturer wants to increase sales or move cars off its
books, and it makes this type of offer to large organizations so their employees can participate.

The manufacturer and SMUD have no business relationship, except the manufacturer has
one location where it is served electricity by SMUD. As a utility, SMUD generally has a mandatory
duty to serve electricity to those wanting it within SMUD's service territory. Other than the
manufacturer having an electric service account with SMUD, SMUD does not anticipate there
being any further relationship in the foreseeable near future between the two entities.

ANALYSIS

A "gift" is defined as any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value
is not received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate
or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to
official status." (Section 82028(a).) A "payment" is defined as a payment, distribution, transfer,
loan, advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services or anything of value,
whether tangible or intangible. (Section 82044.) Based on these definitions, the discount would be a
"gift" unless an exception applies.

In In re Russel (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 191, the Commission clarified when a rebate or discount
in the price of something of value was actually a rebate or discount made in the regular course of
business to members of the public without regard to their official status and excluded from the
definition of gift. The Commission stated:

"After considering the language and purposes of the Political Reform Act, we
conclude that a discount made available on a uniform basis to all state employees is a
discount available to members of the public without regard to official status.
Accordingly, such a discount is not income and is not subject to disclosure under the
Political Reform Act.

"Income disclosure is required so that assets and income of public officials which
may be materially affected by their official actions will be disclosed. Section
81002(d). Discounts are included in the definition of income so that officials who are
offered special reductions when they purchase goods and services will disclose those
transactions for public scrutiny. However, many discounts are offered to all members
of the public and do not create any potential for improper influence. Requiring the
disclosure of all discounts would impose burdensome reporting requirements without
serving a legitimate public purpose. Consequently, the statutory definition of
'income' excludes discounts which are made available to members of the public
without regard to their official status.

"Arguably, the words 'official status' could refer to an official's status as a state
employee. However, the Political Reform Act is concerned with disclosure of
income that may be affected by a public official's 'official actions,' and official
actions are those acts done in one's official capacity under color and by virtue of
one's office. Gunsul v. Ray, 6 Cal. App. 2d 528, 530 (1935). We think that the
purposes of the Political Reform Act are best served by interpreting the words

File No. A-17-138
Page No. 4

'official status' to refer to the capacity in which one performs official actions. We
conclude, therefore, that the 'official status' of state legislators is their status as
elected state officers, not their status as state employees. Consequently, a discount
that is available to all employees of the State of California, without regard to what
office or position they hold in state government, is a discount made available
"without regard to official status.'

"A discount which is not includable as income must not only be made available
"without regard to official status,' it must be made available to 'members of the
public.' The statutory language does not require that the discount be made available
to 'all' members of the public, but implies that the discount will be offered on a
uniform basis to a diverse group. In the circumstances posed by this opinion request,
the discount is made available to all employees of the State of California. This group
is a large and heterogenous assortment of individuals which includes more than
130,000 persons. Because of the size and diversity of the class, we conclude that a
discount available to all state employees is a discount made "available to members of
the public.'"

The Russel opinion did not create explicit standards to determine if a discount was made available
to members of the public without regard to their official status. However, since 1975, we have
consistently considered certain factors:

(1) The group of potential recipients is large. The exception has been applied to as few as
500 (Lum Advice Letter, No. A-98-147) and as large a number as 130,000 persons (In re Russel,
supra; Price Advice Letter, No. A-85-067.) All of SMUD's employees and employees at UC San
Diego, Davis, Merced, San Francisco, and Santa Cruz are eligible to receive the discount. This is a
large group that satisfies the first factor.

(2) The group of potential recipients is diverse. All of SMUD's employees have a chance to
participate in this offer (approximately 2,100 employees) and the offer is not limited to officials
with decision-making authority (only approximately 650 officials who file a Form 700), and all
public employees at the following University of California campuses: San Diego, Davis, Merced,
San Francisco, and Santa Cruz. You noted that the offer is further extended to numerous people
outside of SMUD, some of whom are not even public employees and thus could not be considered
an official. The discount is available to a diverse group of recipients, not limited to high-level
employees.

(3) The availability of the discount is fairly and properly publicized and does not unfairly
benefit higher level public officials. Any SMUD employee can participate in the manufacturer's
offer by going to a participating dealer to pick out the car they like of that particular model. The
employee then shows his or her SMUD employee identification to obtain the discount when
purchasing the car from the dealer. There is nothing in your facts to suggest this factor is not met.

Thus, the exception would apply and the discount would not be considered a gift or income
to recipients.

File No. A-17-138
Page No. 5

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
